questions.question: You want to configure your domain 'mycompany.com' so that emails sent to 'user@mycompany.com' are delivered to 'mail.mycompany.com'. Which DNS record type do you need?
options: A record pointing mycompany.com to mail.mycompany.com
CNAME record aliasing mycompany.com to mail.mycompany.com
MX record for mycompany.com pointing to mail.mycompany.com with a priority
TXT record containing the mail server address
questions.explanation: MX (Mail Exchanger) records control email delivery routing. An MX record for mycompany.com specifies which hostname (mail.mycompany.com) accepts email for that domain, along with a priority number. A records map to IPs, CNAME creates aliases, and TXT holds text data — none of these control email routing.

questions.question: You add a CNAME record: 'www.example.com CNAME example.com'. Now you also want to add an A record for 'www.example.com'. What happens?
options: Both records coexist and clients use whichever they prefer
The A record overrides the CNAME since A records take precedence
This is invalid — a CNAME cannot coexist with any other record type at the same name
The CNAME is ignored for A record queries
questions.explanation: RFC 1034 prohibits a CNAME from coexisting with any other record type at the same owner name. A CNAME says 'this name is an alias for that other name' — it replaces the name entirely. Adding an A record alongside a CNAME creates a contradiction: is this name an alias, or does it have its own IP? DNS implementations must reject this configuration.

questions.question: You query 'dig gmail.com MX +short' and get: '5 gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com.' and '10 alt1.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com.'. Which server receives email FIRST?
options: alt1.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com because 10 > 5 and higher numbers win
gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com because 5 < 10 and lower priority numbers indicate higher preference
Both servers receive equal amounts of email since MX priority is just for display
The server listed first in the response, regardless of priority number
questions.explanation: MX priority is counterintuitively named: a LOWER number means HIGHER preference. The sending mail server will always try the MX record with the lowest priority number first. So gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com (priority 5) is tried before alt1.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com (priority 10). The higher-numbered servers serve as backups if lower-numbered ones are unreachable.

questions.question: You query 'dig example.com TXT' and see: '"v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com -all"'. What does the '-all' at the end mean?
options: All email from this domain is rejected by default
The SPF record applies to all subdomains as well as the apex domain
Any sending server NOT explicitly authorized should be treated as a hard fail (mail is rejected)
The domain has disabled SPF enforcement
questions.explanation: In SPF records, the 'all' mechanism matches any sender not covered by the earlier mechanisms. The qualifier before 'all' controls what happens: '-all' (hardfail) means reject mail from unauthorized servers; '~all' (softfail) means mark as suspicious but accept; '+all' means allow any server (dangerously permissive); '?all' means neutral. '-all' is the strictest setting, telling receiving servers to reject email from any IP not authorized by the SPF record.

questions.question: The dig output for a query contains an 'ADDITIONAL SECTION' with A records for nameservers listed in the 'AUTHORITY SECTION'. What are these additional A records called, and why are they included?
options: Cache records — previously resolved answers pre-fetched to speed up future queries
Glue records — A records for nameservers whose hostnames are within the same zone, preventing a circular dependency when resolving the nameserver's own address
Hint records — suggestions to the client about which servers to query next
Authority records — duplicates of the NS records in IP format for convenience
questions.explanation: Glue records solve a bootstrapping problem: if a nameserver for 'example.com' is 'ns1.example.com', resolving ns1.example.com requires querying the example.com nameservers — but you don't know the IP yet. This is circular. Glue records break the cycle by having the PARENT zone (e.g., .com TLD) include the A record for ns1.example.com directly in the referral response. They appear in the additional section.
